title: Ask Sean Anything Mailbag: ‘Avatar 2,’ 10 Exciting Under-40 Directors, and ‘Everything Everywhere All at Once’ at the Oscars
format: Sean and Bobby open the mailbag: whether Netflix killed cinema, a film-class syllabus on deception, kids' movies, the new 'Avatar' trailer, the hardest Hall of Fame to build, the under-40 directors worth watching, and 'Everything Everywhere All at Once' at the Oscars.

films.title: Beasts of No Nation
films.note: Cited as one of the early Netflix originals that started the killing-cinema conversation.

films.title: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness
films.note: $185M domestic; Sean's evidence the box office is coming back.

films.title: Everything Everywhere All at Once
films.note: Crossing $40M, which excites him: an original genre movie with heart.

films.title: The Lost City
films.note: A merely-okay movie creeping toward $100M, another sign things are back.

segments.heading: The Art of Deception: a film class
segments.note: Justin's question — 10 (well, 11) films Sean would assign

films.title: Kiss Me Deadly
films.note: The pure introduction of irresolution as a storytelling trope.

films.title: Psycho
films.note: Killing off Janet Leigh's star at 40 minutes as the great subversion.

films.title: Persona
films.note: Bergman on identity and swapped roles.

films.title: The Killing
films.note: Kubrick, for the shock of its ending (a cousin to Kiss Me Deadly).

films.title: House of Games
films.note: Mamet's con-men film Sean's father showed him young; distrust everything you see.

films.title: Eyes Wide Shut
films.note: A dream movie about whether any of it is really happening.

films.title: Audition
films.note: Miike's riveting, disgusting nightmare.

films.title: Dogtooth
films.note: Lanthimos on language, tradition, and what a family accepts as fact.

films.title: Memento
films.note: Nolan's reversal of storytelling, worth three weeks of dissection.

films.title: The Sixth Sense
films.note: Shyamalan reframing what Hitchcock did.

films.title: Get Out
films.note: Set a new trajectory in horror-thriller storytelling since the show began.

segments.heading: Truly cinematic movies for a kid under five

films.title: My Neighbor Totoro
films.note: The pick his cinephile friends keep recommending; for Sean, Miyazaki's best.

films.title: Spirited Away
films.note: The other Ghibli some would name over Totoro.

films.title: Princess Mononoke
films.note: Also floated as Miyazaki's best.

films.title: The Wizard of Oz
films.note: Sean has talked often about its influence on him; show it early.

films.title: Bambi
films.note: 'Little April Shower' calms his daughter Alice down.

films.title: Fantasia
films.note: Boring to most kids until you're older (or, per Sean, until you're edible).

films.title: Finding Nemo
films.note: Sweet and funny, and visually distinct for being set entirely underwater.

films.title: Monsters, Inc.
films.note: Bobby's homesick-from-school rewatch alongside Nemo.

films.title: A Bug's Life
films.note: Bobby's socialist read: the ants take power back from the grasshoppers.

segments.heading: The ‘Avatar: The Way of Water’ trailer

films.title: Avatar: The Way of Water
films.note: Trailer just hit YouTube; Sean says you can't judge Avatar off a laptop.

films.title: Avatar
films.note: Sean saw it in IMAX and it worked; Bobby, 12 at the time, found it a tropey Pocahontas.

films.title: Pocahontas
films.note: Bobby's shorthand for Avatar's white-savior story.

films.title: Terminator 2: Judgment Day
films.note: Part of Cameron's doubt-him-at-your-peril track record.

films.title: Titanic
films.note: The 'really?' Cameron swing that became the biggest movie of all time.

films.title: The Northman
films.note: Sean at 40 sees the reinvention of a trope: you're like, Hamlet.

films.title: Licorice Pizza
films.note: The one trailer Sean might have driven out to a theater to catch.

films.title: Top Gun: Maverick
films.note: Bobby's espresso-fueled Dolby plan; Sean warns it's tense.

segments.heading: The hardest Hall of Fame to build
segments.note: TheRunningCAS's question — after the Nic Cage pod

films.title: National Treasure
films.note: The Cage inclusion that made people irrationally angry.

films.title: Con Air
films.note: One of the three big Cage action movies they felt they had to acknowledge.

films.title: The Rock
films.note: Ditto: the mid-'90s action clump.

films.title: Face/Off
films.note: The third one Bobby had to be reminded of.

films.title: The Color of Money
films.note: The Cruise title Chris would fight to include.

segments.heading: What's at the center of culture now?

films.title: E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial
films.note: The kind of movie people once organized leisure time around.

films.title: Terms of Endearment
films.note: Another example of movies as the sun the pop-culture planets orbited.

films.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home
films.note: More culturally powerful than the biggest TV, per Sean: nothing is more mass than movies.

segments.heading: The best under-40 filmmakers
segments.note: Connor's question — pushing back on Sam Esmail's 'no great millennial movie yet'

films.title: Lady Bird
films.note: Gerwig: maybe one that lasts the test of time.

films.title: Fruitvale Station
films.note: Coogler: another that might.

films.title: La La Land
films.note: Chazelle: will do it for some people.

films.title: The Lighthouse
films.note: Eggers: will do it for Sean.

films.title: Uncut Gems
films.note: The Safdies: indisputably, for Sean.

films.title: Shithouse
films.note: Cooper Raiff's Gen-Z first feature; feels like early Linklater.

films.title: Shiva Baby
films.note: Emma Seligman's first feature: Elaine May meets Polanski anxiety.

films.title: Cha Cha Real Smooth
films.note: Raiff's Sundance follow-up.

films.title: Zola
films.note: Janicza Bravo, 41, but a film living between millennial and Gen-Z.

segments.heading: ‘Everything Everywhere All at Once’ at the Oscars

films.title: Everything Everywhere All at Once
films.note: Sean hopes it gets in: a chance to celebrate Ke Huy Quan, Michelle Yeoh, Jamie Lee Curtis, James Hong.

films.title: The Power of the Dog
films.note: An achievement with a small angle of viewership: most people don't watch it.

films.title: Banana Split
films.note: Written by Hannah Marks, whom Sean keeps flagging.

films.title: Mark, Mary & Some Other People
films.note: Marks's okay-but-promising directorial outing.

films.title: Happening
films.note: Audrey Diwan's incredible, hard-to-watch abortion film; Sean saw it at Sundance.

films.title: Nanny
films.note: Nikyatu Jusu's Sundance award-sweeper: domestic thriller with horror edges.

films.title: His House
films.note: Remi Weekes's effective Netflix horror about origins.

films.title: Test Pattern
films.note: Shatara Michelle Ford, one of Sean's favorites of its year.

films.title: The Novice
films.note: Lauren Hadaway, another Sean favorite.

films.title: Pig
films.note: Michael Sarnoski's Cage film; he's now on a Quiet Place prequel.

films.title: A Quiet Place
films.note: The franchise Sarnoski is joining.

films.title: A Quiet Place Part II
films.note: Sean thinks the sequel is underrated.

Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madn.title: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness
